feedback.text: The contribution of the Central European Lawyers Initiative (CELI) is provided in the attached document.

paragraphs.text: FÉDÉRATION FRANÇAISE DES TÉLÉCOMS
Call for evidence
Digital Fairness Act

The French Telecoms Federation (hereinafter 'FFTelecoms') represents electronic communications operators in France. Its mission is to promote an innovative industry that is responsible towards society, the environment, individuals and businesses, to protect the economic interests of the sector and to promote the image of its members and the profession at national and international level. FFTelecoms is a member of EuroISPA, the pan-European association of Internet service providers, which represents more than 2,500 companies.
FFTelecoms is pleased to respond to the call for comments on the future Digital Fairness Act, which is in line with the EU's digital regulatory policy and which calls for the following observations:

Several practices targeted by the Commission's initiative are already covered by European and/or French national law (certain dark patterns, simplified online termination, etc.). FFTelecoms therefore supports the objective of harmonising national practices and legislation and simplifying consumer law at European level, favouring a horizontal rather than a sectoral approach. It advocates for the reduction of redundant obligations, particularly those imposed by the e-commerce directive or the European Electronic Communications Code (EECC), which generate high compliance costs without clear benefits for consumers.

Furthermore, FFTelecoms agrees with the European Commission on the need to take action against the negative externalities of the attention economy, in particular the effects of addictive interfaces on mental health, especially among minors. In this regard, it supports the adoption of measures against certain practices, in particular those mentioned in sections 2 and 3 of the consultation (addictive design and specific features related to video games).

FFTelecoms also insists on the need to incorporate a goal of digital sobriety by limiting certain features (autoplay, infinite scrolling) that consume a lot of bandwidth and have a high environmental impact. It recommends that the European Commission draw inspiration from French initiatives in the field of eco-design of digital services.

However, it opposes extending the responsibility of telecom operators in this area, arguing that only platforms, which are directly behind the practices harmful to consumers targeted by the Commission, should be held responsible.
The Commission must pursue its objective of harmonisation and simplification by favouring a horizontal approach to consumer law regulation
a. FFTelecoms members fully endorse the objective of rationalising and simplifying general consumer law
More specifically, certain measures should be simplified, in particular:

Certain pre-contractual information requirements for consumers – pricing, technical and environmental information – which are becoming increasingly numerous and, due to their quantity and technical nature, are making it increasingly difficult for consumers to understand. These should be simplified in the context of repetitive transactions with the same commercial operator (e.g. in-app purchases) and automated contracts concluded by consumers using a digital assistant (AI).
The consumer's right of withdrawal and/or termination for certain digital content subscription services.

Consumer law should be systematically streamlined to avoid duplication between horizontal and sectoral regulations. This would improve the competitiveness of businesses while ensuring greater transparency for consumers and their rights.
b. Regarding the consultation on the Digital Networks Act, FFTelecoms members consider that a horizontal approach to consumer protection should be preferred to a sectoral approach
Currently, the telecommunications sector is subject not only to general consumer law, but also to special consumer law.

Sector-specific regulation is based on the EECC. In this regard, Articles 102 and 103 lay down pre-contractual and transparency obligations that go well beyond the obligations prescribed under general consumer law. In addition, the 2019 directive has led to a proliferation of often redundant standards that undermine the transparency and clarity of information provided to consumers. Added to this are additional national constraints that operators are sometimes subject to under French law. Finally, all of these obligations represent a high cost for the telecommunications sector in order to comply with the EU standards.

We therefore recommend removing most of the rules that overlap with those of the horizontal regulation, retaining only certain specific provisions that have proven their effectiveness (e.g. Article 105 of the EECC on contract changes and Article 107 of the EECC on bundled offers).

It seems appropriate to review the overall structure of the EU consumer law regulatory framework in order to simplify and harmonise it with a view to striking a satisfactory balance between the obligations incumbent on operators and consumer protection. Such an initiative

must not be undertaken to the detriment of consumer protection, but rather by taking better account of consumers' real and current needs. The principles of necessity and proportionality must guide the European legislator.
To this end, FFTelecoms members call for:
Simplification of the body of consumer law standards (by removing redundant obligations and updating requirements in line with the reality of the digital market); and
A preference for a horizontal framework applicable to consumer law and avoidance of multiple sectoral regulations in this area.

c. Without calling into question the existing regulatory frameworks for several targeted practices, harmonisation of legislation is also necessary, in particular to enable uniform application by the authorities of the various Member States

Several practices targeted by the Commission are already regulated by European law (certain dark patterns such as countdown practices or misleading commercial practices). Nevertheless, it appears necessary to harmonise practices at European level, in particular to standardise the interpretation of texts by national supervisory authorities.

More specifically, the French telecoms regulator FFTelecoms recommends harmonisation on the interpretation and control of unfair commercial practices (short-term offers, comparison prices/promotions).

Digital companies in the attention economy generate negative externalities that require intervention by the European legislator, but this should not imply an increased burden of responsibility for operators
The French telecoms association FFTelecoms supports the European digital regulation policy initiated during the last term of the European Commission.

The Digital Services Act (DSA) and the Digital Markets Act (DMA) are pioneering pieces of legislation, but certain practices that are harmful to consumers and society still escape European regulation. This is particularly the case for certain addictive features ('addictive patterns') against which neither operators nor users are able to take direct action.
As a result, FFTelecoms members consider that further legislative intervention is appropriate for the reasons set out below.
a. FFTelecoms members agree that there are harmful practices that expose consumers, particularly minors, to adverse health consequences

Digital players in the attention economy deploy business models that maximise the capture of users' attention. Numerous studies have shown that the digital attention economy has a negative impact on users' cognitive abilities and could have significant health and economic consequences.

Overexposure to screens and social media use may be associated with poorer sleep quality and a higher prevalence of psychological disorders such as depression, anxiety and stress, the economic effects of which are already being observed in the short term (around 0.6% of French GDP). In the medium term (by 2026), the deterioration in cognitive abilities linked to the attention economy could also have an impact on economic activity of between 1.4 and 2.3 points of French GDP. The attention economy would thus have the effect of reducing worker productivity.

In addition, minors are particularly exposed to certain addictive practices and therefore represent a highly vulnerable population that must be given enhanced protection under EU law. Certain features such as 'autoplay' or automatic content recommendations, which prevent users from conducting their own searches and reduce their ability to 'learn by trying', could be particularly harmful to younger people. In France, the expert commission on the impact of young people's exposure to screens published a report in April 2024 that sets out a number of recommendations aimed at better controlling the digital environment for young people.
Consequently, FFTelecoms members support the Commission's proposals set out in the consultation, in particular on:
measures to prevent commercial operators from using rigged interfaces and other unfair techniques that pressure, deceive and manipulate online consumers;

ways to give consumers greater control over their online experience by tackling addictive design features that encourage consumers, particularly minors, to spend excessive amounts of time and money on online goods and services.

In order to limit the negative effects of the harmful practices mentioned above, the adoption of new binding rules in the form of a 'black list' prohibition seems essential. To this end, it might be appropriate to draw up a list of good and bad practices that are compatible or incompatible with European Union rules and standards. Infinite scrolling and autoplay are practices that should be severely restricted or even removed by default for the most vulnerable populations – minors.
b. The Commission must also promote a model of moderate use

Furthermore, the environmental footprint of digital technology must remain a priority for future EU digital regulation policies, particularly in the context of the Digital Fairness Act, in line with European objectives for ecological transition.

In their contribution to the Digital Networks Act, FFTelecoms members highlighted the critical importance of financing digital infrastructure in the face of exponential growth in content consumption and AI in global internet traffic. Netflix, Alphabet, Amazon, Meta, and Akamai (Apple, HBO, OCS, Twitch, Disney+) account for 51% of incoming traffic on the networks of major operators, and as much as 80% during peak hours. Video-sharing social networks are also very bandwidth-intensive.

Network saturation poses a real risk if this trend in usage continues to grow exponentially, compounded by the development of AI services and applications. Networks are designed to handle bandwidth usage during peak hours, when network traffic reaches its maximum. Capacity deployments have an immediate and consequential environmental cost in terms of energy consumption that network operators cannot offset despite ongoing decarbonisation efforts (equipment tenders favouring eco-designed equipment, putting our equipment on standby, circularity of infrastructure).

To remedy this, FFTelecoms members are calling on the Commission to limit or even ban certain uses in order to promote more moderate usage by requiring digital services to adopt a more neutral design that better protects consumers. This applies in particular to auto-play and scrolling, which significantly increase data consumption and which the Commission could ban under the Digital Fairness Act. Encouraging large platforms to adapt the features and characteristics of their most energy-intensive and bandwidth-consuming services should also be considered through a financial mechanism based on a benchmark for the energy efficiency of digital services.

In June 2023, France published the General Eco-design Reference Framework for Digital Services (RGESN), which lists a set of best practices for digital service providers, with a view to promoting environmental efficiency in the face of strategies designed to capture user attention.

In short, the aim is to limit certain identified practices on the one hand, and to give control back to users on the other, through clear and verified information on the effect of platforms and behavioural patterns directly within applications. But also to:
Limit the capture of data and metadata used for targeted advertising, which reinforces addiction;
Introduce features and tools within the application to control the source and display of information, as well as a procedure for reporting harmful content;
Introduce minimal user interaction features, such as a 'See more/Show more' button, instead of preloading and automatically launching videos.
The RGESN is a good example of regulation, but it remains a non-binding instrument with limited effect on the parties concerned.
At EU level, the Digital Fairness Act would be an appropriate regulatory lever for creating the kind of digital fairness that protects consumers, which is what FFTelecoms members are calling for.
c. However, the Digital Fairness Act must not lead to an expansion of operators' responsibilities with regard to targeted practices
The focus should be on those actors in the value chain who are directly involved in the harmful practices mentioned above, namely online platforms and, in particular, social networks.

Conversely, it should be underlined that the role of internet service providers is limited to that of a simple carrier or technical intermediary under the principle of net neutrality to which they are subject.

Thus, in line with the graduated approach of the Digital Services Act, the future obligations of the Digital Fairness Act should be proportionate and focused on those actors whose practices cause harm to consumers.
